coredata.dc:description: The enormous upsurge in the hospital acquired (nosocomial) infections have entailed the need of an alternative strategy to combat fungal infections. In particular, infections caused by Candida auris represent a serious threat to patients due to their ability to develop multidrug resistance inside human body. Here, we report the synthesis of nitrogen-doped carbon dots (N-doped C-dots) from the rhizomes of Trillium govanianum to investigate their structure–activity relationship in inhibiting biofilm formation in three Candida auris isolates: C. auris NCCPF-470203, C. auris NCCPF-470200, and C. auris NCCPF-470197. The structural properties of C-dots were theoretically analysed via DFT simulations and further characterised through various characterization techniques. The current study addresses the existing gap by evaluating the antifungal potential of biomass-derived N-doped carbon dots and offer a safer and more sustainable alternative to conventional chemical-based antifungal agents. In relation to this, the antioxidant study of C-dots was evaluated which revealed a concentration dependent increase in RSA values, exhibiting a progressive increment from 5.87 to 14.92 %. Additionally, the fungicidal potential of C-dots was estimated against three different fungal strains, demonstrating an optimal MFC and MIC values for each fungal strains, equivalent to 1.25 mg/mL and 0.625 mg/mL respectively. Comparing the antifungal potential of all the three fungal strains, the best biofilm inhibitory activity was found against C. auris NCCPF-470203 strain. The mechanistic view behind the antifungal properties was further elucidated through theoretical analysis and molecular docking studies. Overall, the outcomes of the present study point towards the utility of C-dots for preventing and controlling infections caused by pathogenic fungal strains.

head.ce:title: Engineering fungicidal nitrogen-doped carbon dots from Trillium govanianum rhizomes: A theoretical and experimental approach against Candida auris

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: The enormous upsurge in the hospital acquired (nosocomial) infections have entailed the need of an alternative strategy to combat fungal infections. In particular, infections caused by Candida auris represent a serious threat to patients due to their ability to develop multidrug resistance inside human body. Here, we report the synthesis of nitrogen-doped carbon dots (N-doped C-dots) from the rhizomes of Trillium govanianum to investigate their structure–activity relationship in inhibiting biofilm formation in three Candida auris isolates: C. auris NCCPF-470203, C. auris NCCPF-470200, and C. auris NCCPF-470197. The structural properties of C-dots were theoretically analysed via DFT simulations and further characterised through various characterization techniques. The current study addresses the existing gap by evaluating the antifungal potential of biomass-derived N-doped carbon dots and offer a safer and more sustainable alternative to conventional chemical-based antifungal agents. In relation to this, the antioxidant study of C-dots was evaluated which revealed a concentration dependent increase in RSA values, exhibiting a progressive increment from 5.87 to 14.92 %. Additionally, the fungicidal potential of C-dots was estimated against three different fungal strains, demonstrating an optimal MFC and MIC values for each fungal strains, equivalent to 1.25 mg/mL and 0.625 mg/mL respectively. Comparing the antifungal potential of all the three fungal strains, the best biofilm inhibitory activity was found against C. auris NCCPF-470203 strain. The mechanistic view behind the antifungal properties was further elucidated through theoretical analysis and molecular docking studies. Overall, the outcomes of the present study point towards the utility of C-dots for preventing and controlling infections caused by pathogenic fungal strains.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: The persistent threat of impenetrable biofilm formation and the chronic wound infections caused by nosocomial fungal pathogens have increasingly endangered human life and significantly increased mortality rates globally [1,2]. The persistent emergence of fungal resistance against therapeutics has posed a continuous threat to global public health, particularly in the context of rationally designed biomedical equipment that fails to counteract fungal pathogens effectively [3]. Despite advancements in therapeutic drugs, their efficacy is being undermined by the rising prevalence of nosocomial infections caused by highly pathogenic fungal species [4,5]. These infections, commonly referred to as hospital-acquired infections, remain a recurring issue and are predominantly observed in surgical wards, medical units, and intensive care settings [6,7]. In recent years, the most prevalent and invasive nosocomial infections have been associated with Candida species, affecting approximately 1.7 billion people worldwide [8]. Recognizing the severity of the issue, the World Health Organization has classified several Candida species, including Candida albicans, Candida auris, and Candida glabrata, as priority pathogenic yeasts [9,10]. Among these, Candida auris has emerged as a particularly alarming fungal pathogen due to its multidrug-resistant nature and its frequent outbreaks in healthcare settings [11]. This species is exceptionally challenging to treat, as it exhibits virulence factors such as biofilm formation which is a complex multicellular community encapsulated within a self-secreted extracellular matrix and protects the pathogen from therapeutic agents. The escalating prevalence of Candida auris infections has prompted significant interest in the development of novel nanomaterials specifically designed to inhibit biofilm formation and mitigate fungal infections [12]. Nowadays, metal- and metal oxide-based nanotherapeutics have been widely explored for their medical and pharmaceutical applications due to their potent antimicrobial properties [13,14]. However, despite their promising therapeutic potential, these metallic nanoparticles often induce nonspecific interactions with biological systems and limits their practical applications in healthcare.

In this context, carbon dots (C-dots), a novel class of zero-dimensional, fluorescent carbon-based nanomaterials, have gained considerable attention for their broad-spectrum antibacterial and antifungal properties [15–17]. Due to their remarkable biocompatibility, tunable surface functionalities, and environmentally friendly synthesis, C-dots have emerged as a superior alternative to conventional metal- and metal oxide-based nanomaterials [18]. Moreover, the utilization of natural precursors, such as plant biomass and plant-derived extracts, in the biosynthesis of C-dots significantly enhances their physicochemical and biological properties [19–22]. While nanomaterial-based approaches have been extensively studied for Candida albicans, Candida auris remains comparatively underexplored in this context. Therefore, employing plant-based precursors for synthesising C-dots offers a facile and advantageous strategy to combat infections caused by Candida auris while minimizing side effects and reducing the environmental impact associated with traditional synthetic methods [23,24].

Medicinal plants, rich in bioactive compounds with anti-inflammatory and antifungal properties, provide an eco-friendly, cost-effective, and sustainable approach to developing carbonaceous nanomaterials [25]. A trans-himalayan medicinal plant, Trillium govanianum, holds therapeutic potential as root extract of this plant has antifungal, anti-inflammatory, anticancer, analgesic, and wound healing properties. Further, the presence of secondary metabolites such as phenols, terpenoids, tannins, saponins, flavonoids act as effective herbal ingredients to aid in different ailments. Owing to its significant use in traditional Himalayan medicine and its rich phytochemical profile, Trillium govanianum represents a valuable and underexplored natural resource. These attributes, along with its high carbon content, make it a promising and sustainable precursor for the green synthesis of carbon dots. In this work we have reported the synthesis of C-dots from rhizomes of Trillium govanianum via thermal calcination procedure. The structural features of the developed N-doped C-dots was theoretically analysed using Density Functional Theory. The C-dots were devoid of any physical and chemical surface functionalization and were further utilized for determining the antifungal efficacy against the pathogenic clinical isolates of Candida auris. Intriguingly, N-doped C-dots featured notably enhanced antifungal activity against Candida auris which manifest the flexible conversion of medicinal plant extract into valuable product. The mechanism underlying the fungicidal properties of N-doped C-dots was further investigated with in silico molecular docking simulations. More importantly, N-doped C-dots exhibited an enhanced antifungal and antioxidant properties and increased biofilm inhibition with satisfactory results in biological applications like bandages and band-aids for managing localized infections. This study paves a novel way for the fabricating of potential antifungal agents from medicinal plants for biomedical applications.

Trillium govanianum rhizomes were collected from botanical garden, Himachal Pradesh, India. All glassware and PTFE-coated stirrer bars employed in the nanoparticle synthesis experiments were thoroughly cleaned by soaking in aqua regia for a minimum of 2 h, followed by extensive rinsing with deionized water. Millipore Milli-Q purified water was used for all experimental procedures to ensure high purity and minimize contamination.

The fungal cultures used in this study were C. auris NCCPF-470197, C. auris NCCPF-470200, C. auris NCCPF-470203. These cultures were procured from the National Culture Collection of Pathogenic Fungi (NCCPF), Department of Medical Microbiology, Postgraduate Institute of Medical Education and Research (PGIMER), Chandigarh, India.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Synthesis of N-doped C-dots from Trillium govanianum
ce:section.ce:para: The N-doped C-dots were synthesized via a one-step calcination method. The synthesis process began with 5 g of rhizomes from the trans-Himalayan plant Trillium govanianum. These rhizomes were thoroughly washed with 50 % ethanolic solution to remove impurities, followed by a final rinse with double-distilled water. The cleaned rhizomes were then oven-dried at 80 °C to eliminate residual moisture and ground into a fine powder using an electric mortar and pestle. The powdered rhizomes were transferred into a silica crucible and subjected to calcination at 300 °C for 2.5 h [26–28]. After the calcination process, 0.5 g of the resultant ash were dispersed in 500 mL of water to remove impurities and ensure uniform particle size distribution. The dispersion was stirred continuously for 24 h to facilitate thorough mixing. Subsequently, the solution was centrifuged at 10,000 rpm, utilizing centrifuge tubes fitted with microfilters to isolate the nanoparticles. The collected supernatant was further purified through dialysis using a dialysis bag with a molecular weight cut-off (MWCO) of 10,000 Da. The final C-dots exhibited a bright blue emission under 365 nm UV light and were dried for future applications. This synthesis approach effectively yielded high-quality N-doped C-dots with uniform size and bright luminescence.

The thermal calcination process for synthesizing N-doped C-dots was conducted using an AICIL muffle furnace. The size and crystal structure of the C-dots were analyzed via transmission electron microscopy (TEM) and high-resolution transmission electron microscopy (HRTEM) using a Hitachi H-7500 electron microscope operating at 100 kV. The UV–visible absorption spectra were obtained using a Jasco V-530 UV–Vis spectrophotometer within a wavelength range of 200–800 nm, while photoluminescence (PL) measurements were carried out using a Perkin Elmer LS 55 spectrophotometer with an excitation bandwidth of 10 nm, emission bandwidth of 10 nm, and a scan speed of 200 nm/min. Sample stirring and heating were performed using an IKA C-MAG HS7 magnetic stirrer equipped with a heating plate to ensure uniform mixing and thermal treatment. The Fourier-transform infrared (FT-IR) spectra were recorded using a Perkin Elmer RX1 spectrometer in the range of 4000–400 cm−1 to identify functional groups. The morphology and elemental composition of the C-dots were examined using a JEOL JSM-7500F Field Emission Scanning Electron Microscope (FESEM) coupled with energy-dispersive X-ray spectroscopy (EDX), while the atomic and elemental composition was analyzed using X-ray Photoelectron Spectroscopy (XPS) with a PHI 5000 Versa Probe III instrument. The crystal structure was evaluated using an X-ray diffractometer (Panalytical X’Pert Pro) with a scan rate of 2 deg. min−1 and monochromatic CuKα radiation over a 2θ range of 5°–90°. Thermal stability and decomposition behavior were assessed using thermogravimetric analysis (TGA) with an SDT Q-600 instrument and a differential scanning calorimeter (DSC) (METTLER TOLEDO Q20). The zeta potential of the C-dots was measured using an Anton Paar Zeta Potential Analyzer to determine the particle charge distribution in the solution. This combination of analytical techniques provided comprehensive insights into the structural, optical, and thermal properties of the synthesized N-doped C-dots.

The pathogenicity or cell surface hydrophobicity of Candida auris was evaluated using the method described by Rosenberg et al. [29]. An overnight grown culture of C. auris was harvested by centrifugation at 8000 rpm for 10 min, followed by subsequent washing with 0.1 M phosphate-buffered saline (PBS). The cells were then resuspended in PBS to a concentration of 106 cells/mL, and the initial optical density (OD) at 600 nm (AU) was recorded. Equal volumes of the cell suspension were mixed with xylene (a nonpolar solvent), chloroform (a monopolar acidic solvent), and hexane (a monopolar basic solvent). The mixtures were incubated at room temperature for 10 min, after which the aqueous phase was carefully separated, and the optical density at 600 nm (AU) was measured again. This procedure enabled the evaluation of C. auris cell surface hydrophobicity based on the interaction with different solvents. The cell surface hydrophobicity % is measured as follows:

Here, A0 represents the initial absorbance while A1 shows the final absorbance of the sample.

To assess the biofilm formation tendency of Candida auris, a well-known phenotypic method was employed using 96-well flat-bottom microtiter plates. Briefly, each well was filled with 100 µL of Sabouraud Dextrose Broth (SDB) media, followed by the addition of 100 µL of log-phase C. auris cultures (106 cells/mL). The plates were incubated at 37 °C for 24, 48, and 72 h. After each incubation period, the biofilms were fixed by adding 100 µL of 99 % methanol for 10–15 min, and the wells were washed twice with sterile normal saline to remove planktonic cells. The biofilms were then air-dried after being rinsed with running water to eliminate excess stain. Subsequently, the biofilms were stained with crystal violet, and the stain was dissolved by adding 100 µL of 33 % glacial acetic acid. The dissolved stain was transferred to a fresh microtiter plate, and absorbance was measured at 560 nm to quantify biofilm development.

The antifungal efficacy of Trillium govanianum crude extract and Trillium govanianum derived N-doped C-dots was evaluated against clinical isolates of Candida auris using round-bottom 96-well sterile plates to determine the minimum inhibitory concentration (MIC) and minimum fungicidal concentration (MFC) [30,31]. In the 96-well microtiter plate assay, 100 µL of Sabouraud Dextrose Broth (SDB) media was added to each well. In the first well, 100 µL of Trillium govanianum crude extract (256 mg/mL) and Trillium govanianum N-doped C-dots (5 mg/mL) were added, followed by two-fold serial dilutions down to a 1:256 dilution. The last three rows served as controls: positive controls with medium, microorganism, and antifungal agent; blanks with medium only; and negative controls with media and the organism. Then, 6 µL of a log-phase culture containing C. auris (106 cells/mL) was added to each dilution, and the plate was incubated overnight at 37 °C. After incubation, 0.1 % resazurin was added to each well and incubated for 1 h. Finally, the color changes were observed to assess microbial growth.

To assess the antifungal activity of chemically synthesized C-dots against C. auris clinical isolates, 100 µL of SDB media was added to each well of a microtiter plate, followed by 100 µL of chemically synthesized C-dots (5 mg/mL) in the first well, which was serially two-fold diluted down to a 1:256 dilution. The last two rows of the plate served as controls: a blank with media only and a negative control with media and the microorganism. After adding 6 µL of C. auris culture (106 cells/mL) to each well, the plate was incubated overnight at 37 °C. Following the incubation period, 0.1 % resazurin was added, and fungal growth inhibition was evaluated based on colorimetric measurements.

The antibiofilm potential of green-synthesized N-doped C-dots was evaluated using the crystal violet assay on a flat-bottom 96-well microtiter plate. A two-fold dilution series of C-dots was prepared across the first nine wells of the plate by adding 100 µL of SDB medium to each well, followed by the addition of C-dots. A separate well containing Fluconazole served as the positive control for antifungal activity (Supporting information section S.2.2). Subsequently, 6 µL of a Candida auris log-phase culture (106 cells/mL) was added to each well, and the plate was incubated overnight at 37 °C. After incubation, the media was decanted, and the biofilm was air-dried. The biofilm was washed twice with PBS buffer to remove planktonic cells and air-dried again. It was then fixed with 99 % methanol for 15 min and stained with 0.1 % crystal violet for 15 min. After staining, the biofilm was washed with running water to remove excess stain and air-dried. Finally, the stained biofilm was dissolved in 33 % glacial acetic acid, transferred to a fresh microtiter plate, and the absorbance was measured at 560 nm to quantify biofilm formation. The percent biofilm inhibition was calculated using Equation (2);

The DPPH assay was used to assess the antioxidant activity of N-doped C-dots [32]. Different concentration of C-dots (1 mg/mL, 1.5 mg/mL, 2.0 mg/mL, and 2.5 mg/mL) were prepared in 0.5 mM DPPH solution in 96 % ethanol. After mixing 100 µL of C-dots with 3 mL of DPPH solution, the mixture was incubated for 30 min at 37 °C to evaluate the color change. After incubation, the absorbance of each solution was measured at 517 nm using a spectrophotometer. With ethanol and DPPH as the control and C-dots in the test solution, the radical scavenging activity (%RSA) was computed using the formula:

ce:section.ce:section-title: Biomedical application of N-doped C-dots on bandages and band-aid
Coating of N-doped C-dots on bandages and band-aid
ce:section.ce:para: Bandages and band-aids were coated with N-doped C-dots in a 12-well microtiter plate that was sterilized for 30 min under UV-C light [33]. Six bandages and six band-aids were placed into the designated wells, and then coated with 10 mg/mL of C-dots. The coating was allowed to dry completely in a hot air oven at 42 °C. This process was repeated three times to ensure a consistent and effective coating on the materials.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Antifungal assay of N-doped C-dots coated bandage and band-aid
ce:section.ce:para: 500 µL of media was added to each well of a 12-well microtiter plate, which was then inoculated with 106 cells/mL of Candida auris culture. Band-aids and bandages were added to the wells in duplicates. The plates were kept for incubation at 37 °C for 24 h. After the incubation period, a resazurin assay was performed to evaluate the antifungal activity of the C-dots coated bandages and band-aids, assessing the growth inhibition of Candida auris in response to the treatment [34].

The calculations in this study were completed with Density Functional Theory (DFT) using Gaussian 09 W suite of programs [35]. The model structure of N-doped C-dots was designed in ChemDraw 23.0.1 software. Geometric optimizations and computational calculations were performed using the B3LYP functional with the 6-311G* basis set. The optimized molecular structures of C-dots were simulated, and the energy level distribution of the lowest unoccupied molecular orbital (LUMO) and the highest occupied molecular orbital (HOMO) was determined with an iso-value of 0.02. The visualization of HOMO and LUMO was performed using GaussView 5 software.

Molecular docking studies of the synthesized N-doped C-dots were conducted against the target structures of ALS3 (adhesin), HSP90 (nucleotide binding domain), and AIP5 (Actin Interacting Protein) of Candida auris. The 3D crystal structures of ALS3 (PDB ID: 4LE8), HSP90 (PDB ID: 6CJI), and AIP5 (PDB ID: 6M4C) were retrieved from the RCSB Protein Data Bank. Docking studies were carried out using Autodock Tools 1.5.7. Prior to docking, all protein structures were optimized by removing water molecules, adding polar H-atoms, assigning Kollman & Gasteiger charges, and applying AD4. The optimal docking complex was identified by analysing the configuration with the lowest energy among all docked poses. The molecular docking analysis was visualized and analyzed using BIOVIA Discovery Studio Visualizer. During the docking process, all protein structures were kept rigid, while only the ligand atoms (representing N-doped C-dots) were allowed to remain flexible [36].

All experiments were performed in triplicate, and the mean values were calculated, plotted, and tabulated. Statistical analysis involved paired comparisons between the control and test groups, with P < 0.001 considered statistically significant [37]. The antifungal susceptibility of Candida auris biofilms was further assessed using the XTT reduction assay, as described in detail in Supporting Information Section S.1.1 and S.2.3.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Synthesis and characterization of N-doped C-dots
ce:section.ce:para:
The stable and monodispersed N-doped C-dots were synthesised via thermal calcination process using rhizomes of Trillium govanianum plant (Fig. 1 a). The N-doped C-dots is known possess enhanced surface reactivity and biological interactions due to presence of electron-rich nature, which significantly improve the potential of C-dots for biological applications [38,39]. Initially, the stability of the synthesised C-dots was evaluated under physiologically relevant saline conditions and prolonged UV light exposure (detailed in the Supporting Information). A systematic DFT studies was employed to investigate the electronic structure, reactivity, and stability of developed sensor (N-doped C-dots) alongside the experimental calculations. Frontier molecular orbital analysis was used to demonstrate the delocalization of electrons between valence band (HOMO) and conduction band (LUMO). The energy gap between the molecular orbitals was represented by the distance between both the valence and conduction band and is given as:
(6) ΔE = E LUMO- EHOMO

Where ΔE represents the energy difference between the HOMO and LUMO orbitals, E LUMO and EHOMO is the energy association with LUMO and HOMO orbitals respectively. From Fig. 1bi-bii, it could be seen that the HOMO of the C-dots is found positioned at −0.178 eV while LUMO was observed at −0.130 eV. Typically, the molecule possesses an energy difference of 0.047 eV. It is anticipated from the energy band gap value that the molecule is kinetically less stable and possess high chemical reactivity with higher probability of intermolecular charge transfer. The surface charge distribution in C-dots were also represented in form of Mulliken charge in Fig. 1(biii). Further the electronic cloud distribution of HOMO and LUMO were represented by molecular electrostatic potential (MEP) maps, shown in Fig. 1biv. The pictograph represents the presence of different sites (red and blue) that are susceptible to electrophilic and nucleophilic attack. The area highlighted in red represents increased electron cloud, hence favoured for electrophilic attack while the area with blue color signifies lesser electron cloud which is favoured location for nucleophilic attack.

The formation of highly luminescent C-dots was primarily confirmed with the UV–vis. and photoluminescence spectroscopy. A typical UV absorption peak of C-dots was observed at 254 nm and 363 nm attributed to π-π* transition of C=C bond in sp2 hybridisation and n-π* transition of C=O and C–N bond respectively (Fig. 2 ai). Subsequently, the photoluminescence spectra were recorded for the C-dots between the wavelength range of 260 to 700 nm.

As shown in Fig. 2aii, the fluorescence excitation spectrum exhibits an obvious excitation dependent fluorescence emission with an optimal excitation at 260 nm and emission at 420 nm. The cumulative overlap between the fluorescence excitation spectrum and the UV absorption band indicates that the absorptions are associated with the same type of transitions responsible for the emission, specifically those involving aromatic structures, such as π → π* transitions.

The quantum yield of C-dots was estimated by comparing the fluorescence intensity of C-dots with the fluorescence intensity of reference i.e. Quinine Sulfate [40]. The calculated Quantum yield of N-doped C-dots was found equivalent to 40.15 %. Further, the CIE (Commission Internationale de L 'Eclairage) coordinate plot represents a transition from blue region with the excitation at 260 nm (CIE coordinates: 0.27,0.18) to green region with excitation of 400 nm (CIE coordinates:0.13, 0.22) (Fig. 2aiii).

The FTIR spectrum of C-dots showed multiple peaks in the range of 500–4000 cm−1, consistent with the structure of C-dots. The FTIR bands at 3160 cm−1 corresponds to O–H and N–H stretching vibrations while the peak at 1405 cm−1 along with a shoulder peak at 1496 cm−1 were attributed to the –NH2 and –COOH group respectively (Fig. 2bi). Notably the bending vibrations for both –OH and C-O group were observed at 1087 and 870 cm−1. A characteristic stretching vibration peak centered at 610 cm−1 represents the presence of C-N group in structure of C-dots [41]. Furthermore, the XRD profile of the N-doped C-dots revealed that the particles possess high crystallinity which is evidenced by the presence of sharp diffraction peaks. In Fig. 2bii, the XRD spectrum displays several strong and high intensity diffraction peaks at 29.7°, 40.4°, 48.4°, and 58.3° corresponding to (002), (100), (102), and (103) planes respectively [42]. Overall, the FTIR and XRD results justifies a crystalline and highly functionalized surface of carbon core. Additionally, the degree of graphitization of C-dots was affirmed by obtaining the Raman Spectra scanned in the range of 50 to 3000 cm−1, shown in Fig. 2biii. The spectra represent the appearance of D band at 1340 cm−1 corresponding to the disordered structure of carbon and G band positioned at 1595 cm−1 attributed to the sp2 hybrid carbon graphitization structure. Further, the relative ID/IG intensity was found equivalent to 1.04. The structure and morphological characteristics of C-dots was further determined by HRTEM analysis (Fig. 2c). The HRTEM results reveal that the obtained C-dots possess quasi-spherical appearance with an average diameter of 4.5 nm (Fig. 2cii). Further, the high-resolution imaging confirmed that the lattice fringes show an interplanar spacing of 0.21 nm corresponding to the (100) plane of graphitic carbon. As illustrated in Fig. 2ciii, the distinct diffracting and diffused crystalline planes in SAED pattern represents polycrystalline nature of C-dots.

The thermogravimetric analysis of the prepared C-dots was performed and their weight loss was recorded as a function of temperature. The TGA and DTA curve of C-dots illustrated two step degradation profile. The first degradation onset occurred around 100° C due to loss of water molecules or dehydration of surface adsorbed water molecules (Fig. 3 ai) [43]. These adsorbed water molecules were bounded by intermolecular H-bonding with functional groups present on the surface of C-dots. The mass loss in the second step was due to the decomposition of the surface functional groups which was represented by a clear DTG peak in Fig. 3ai. Further, the DSC thermogram of C-dots represents an endothermic peak positioned at 314.7° C (Fig. 3aii). The TGA and DSC assessments conclude that the as-synthesised C-dots were thermally stable up to the temperature 650° C which confirms the high thermal stability of C-dots. Additionally, the stability of C-dots was analysed in aqueous medium by measuring the zeta potential values. The results justify a stable and negative zeta potential of −26.8 mV (Fig. 3aiii).

The elemental composition of N-doped C-dots clearly demonstrates the distribution of carbon, oxygen and nitrogen, as illustrated in Fig. 3bi. Moreover, the in-depth investigation of chemical composition and surface states of C-dots was done via obtaining XPS profile. As expected, the XPS survey scan of N-doped C-dots demonstrates three predominant peaks at 284.4, 399.4, and 531.2 eV indicating main elements like carbon, oxygen and nitrogen (Fig. 3bii). The high-resolution spectrum of C1s represents two peak components positioned at 284.4 and 285.5 eV corresponding to C–C bond of sp3 hybridised carbon and C-O bond for carbonyl group respectively (Fig. 3biii). On detailed examination of deconvoluted N1s spectrum, two main peaks centered at 398.8 and 400.6 eV were observed which were ascribed to C-N and N–H bond respectively (Fig. 3biv). In addition, the high resolution O1s spectrum was resolved into two peaks positioned at the binding energies of 530.9 and 532 eV which corresponds to the C-OH and C-O-C bonds respectively (Fig. 3biv). Further, the atomic percentages calculated from the XPS survey spectrum were 67.3 % for carbon, 31.5 % for oxygen, and 1.2 % for nitrogen, confirming successful nitrogen doping of the carbon dots [39,44].

The cell surface hydrophobicity assay conducted on various clinical isolates of Candida auris (NCCPF-470197, NCCPF-470200, and NCCPF-470203) revealed distinct levels of interaction with hydrocarbon solvents of varying polarity, including xylene (non-polar), chloroform (monopolar), and hexane (non-polar) [45]. The observed variation in hydrophobicity among the isolates demonstrates the diverse pathogenic characteristics of C. auris. Specifically, C. auris NCCPF-470197 exhibited strong interaction in moderately polar environments, showing the highest hydrophobicity in chloroform (Fig. 4 a). In contrast, C. auris NCCPF-470200 displayed the greatest hydrophobicity in the non-polar solvent xylene, suggesting a surface composition optimized for fully non-polar interactions. Meanwhile, C. auris NCCPF-470203 demonstrated consistently high hydrophobicity across all tested solvents, indicating a versatile and adaptable surface that facilitates interaction with a wide range of hydrophobic environments (Fig. 4a). These strain-specific variations highlight the differential potential of C. auris isolates to adhere to host surfaces and form biofilms. The findings from the cell surface hydrophobicity assay for the tested isolates are summarized in Table 1 .

The ability of Candida auris isolates (NCCPF-470197, NCCPF-470200, and NCCPF-470203) to form biofilms was assessed using 96-well flat-bottom microtiter plates. A total of 100 µL of C. auris culture in the logarithmic growth phase (106 cells/mL) was inoculated into each well containing SDB medium and incubated at 37 °C for 24, 48, and 72 h. The assay provided valuable insights into the dynamics of biofilm development across different incubation periods (Fig. 4b). A progressive increase in biofilm formation was observed for all three isolates with extended incubation times. Among the isolates, C. auris NCCPF-470203 exhibited the highest biofilm-forming potential, characterized by consistently elevated metabolic activity and biomass up to 72 h (Table 2 ). Furthermore, C. auris NCCPF-470203 isolate demonstrated superior biofilm formation compared to the others, highlighting its robust capacity for sustained metabolic activity and biomass production during biofilm development.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Quantitative antifungal analysis of biological and Chemical-Based C-dots
Minimum inhibitory concentration (MIC) and minimum fungicidal concentration (MFC) assay
ce:section.ce:para: A comparative analysis of MIC and MFC values of Trillium govanianum derived C-dots with other biomass derived carbon dots like Aegle marmelos leaves, Ocimum sanctum flowers, and tobacco leaves has also been presented in Table S1 (Supporting information). The results demonstrate the superior antifungal efficacy of Trillium govanianum-derived carbon dots, which can be attributed to their enriched phytochemical composition and enhanced surface functionalization.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Biofilm inhibition assay of Trillium govanianum derived N-doped C-dots
ce:section.ce:para: In the follow up experiment, the inhibition of Candida auris biofilm formation was evaluated using the crystal violet assay in a 96-well microtiter plate. The percentage of biofilm inhibition was assessed at varying concentrations (0.004 mg/mL to 0.625 mg/mL) of N-doped C-dots with fluconazole as positive control (Supporting information section S.2.2). As shown in Fig. 6 ai, the results indicated that C. auris NCCPF-470203 exhibited the highest susceptibility to inhibition by C-dots across all concentrations, making it the most sensitive strain. In contrast, C. auris NCCPF-470197 demonstrated strong inhibition, although slightly higher concentrations of C-dots were required to achieve comparable effectiveness. Furthermore, C. auris NCCPF-470200 displayed the least inhibition across all tested concentrations, suggesting that it is the least susceptible strain to C-dots in terms of biofilm inhibition. This significant difference may be attributed to the formation of a denser and more compact biofilm by C. auris NCCPF-470200, which restricts the penetration of C-dots into the fungal cells, thereby limiting their antifungal activity and the induction of reactive oxygen species (ROS). In contrast, C. auris NCCPF-470197 and NCCPF-470203 may produce less dense biofilms, allowing for more effective C-dots penetration and enhanced antifungal efficacy. Overall, the results revealed a declining trend in biofilm inhibition with increasing dilution of Trillium govanianum C-dots across all three C. auris isolates (Fig. 6ai).

The antioxidant potential of green-synthesized N-doped C-dots measured at varying concentrations of C-dots (1 mg/mL, 1.5 mg/mL, 2.0 mg/mL, and 2.5 mg/mL), demonstrated a concentration-dependent increase in radical scavenging activity (RSA). The results showed that the RSA values exhibited a progressive increment from 5.87 % to 14.92 % with the enhancement in the C-dots concentration from 1 mg/mL to 2.5 mg/mL. Specifically, RSA values were recorded as 5.87 % for 1 mg/mL, 6.3 % for 1.5 mg/mL, 7.64 % for 2 mg/mL, and 14.92 % for 2.5 mg/mL (Fig. 6aii). Additionally, the IC50 values were calculated for the different concentrations of C-dots and are summarized in Table 4 . The outcomes suggest that at lower concentrations (1 to 1.5 mg/mL), the RSA was relatively low. However, a marked increase in antioxidant activity was observed as the concentration increased to 2 mg/mL to 2.5 mg/mL. The findings suggest the enhanced antioxidant potential of Trillium govanianum derived N-doped C-dots at higher concentrations. However, when compared to the reference antioxidant ascorbic acid, the radical scavenging activity of the C-dots was significantly lower (Fig. S3). Ascorbic acid demonstrated potent antioxidant activity in the DPPH assay at relatively low concentrations, with the RSA values approaching a saturation point at approximately 2 mg/mL. The saturation point suggests that further increases in ascorbic acid concentration do not substantially augment its free radical scavenging capacity. This suggests that, in addition to radical scavenging activity, C-dots may exert their antifungal effects through alternative mechanistic pathways [48–51].

The primitive cause for the emergence and spread of C. auris lies in its ability to persist on distinct biomedical and metallic surfaces, making it a causitive agent for the emergence of nosocomial infections. The current use of C-dots as disinfectants and in band-aid coatings remains limited, as highlighted in several literature reports [52]. To address this gap and explore a practical approach for utilizing biologically synthesized C-dots, bandages and band-aids were coated with C-dots to inhibit C. auris biofilm formation on biomedical and healthcare products commonly found in hospital settings. For this experiment, the resazurin assay was employed using a concentration of 10 mg/mL of C-dots. Subsequently, the C-dots were uniformly coated onto bandages and band-aids in three successive layers. Prior to coating, the C-dots were sonicated at a frequency of 40 kHz for 5 min, with alternating 30-second intervals of sonication and rest. The in vitro assessment revealed a differential antifungal efficacy between the two substrates. Notably, bandages exhibited comparatively lower antifungal activity, as indicated by a light pink color change (Fig. 6b). In contrast, the band-aids demonstrated significantly higher antifungal activity, evident by a blue/violet color change, suggesting greater inhibition of Candida auris. These results highlight the enhanced performance of C-dots-coated band-aids in combating fungal infections. A mechanistic overview of ROS generation by C-dots and its role in inhibiting C. auris biofilm formation is illustrated in Fig. 7 .

For the development of effective therapeutics, it is crucial to explore the potential involvement of C-dots as a fungicidal agent for evoking their antifungal potential. A detailed understanding of the disruption of cellular processes through the antagonism of key enzymatic targets was explored using in silico molecular docking simulations, in accordance with the methodology reported by Kumar et al. (2023). [53]. This approach accounts for the conformation variation and provides information about the binding energy, inhibition constant along with the non-covalent interactions of ligands molecules to the targeted protein. The simulation of the binding interaction between the C-dots and target proteins (ALS3, HSP90 and AIP5) of Candida auris are illustrated in Fig. 8 . The 2D structure obtained from the docking studies show that C-dots bind with THR 296A, LEU 293A, TRP 295A, SER 170A, ARG 299A, THR 168A in case of 4LE8 with a docking score of −6.59 kcal/mol (Fig. 8a).

Fig. 8b represents C-dots docked into the binding pockets of nucleotide binding domain HSP90. It is anticipated that the binding is facilitated by interaction of C-dots with VAL 212A, GLU 190A, LYS 194A, GLN 209A, LEU 210A, VAL 211A in 6CJI enzyme of Candida auris. This interaction resulted in excellent binding score of −6.1 kcal/mol. Furthermore, another chosen protein 6M4C essential for cellular responses and responsible for regulation of several actin filaments represents high binding energy with C-dots i.e. −7.16 kcal/mol. In 6M4C, interactions were found between C-dots and 7 residues namely GLU 44A, PRO 12A, LYS 42A, VAL 13A, GLY 40A, TYR 97A, SER 10A (Fig. 8c). As the non-covalent interaction only contributes towards high affinity between ligand and the target protein while the hydrogen bonding provides strong binding affinity between them. In all the three docking studies, the hydrogen bonding was predominantly greater in case of 6M4C which resulted in highest binding energy (−7.16 kcal/mol) followed by 4LE8 (−6.59 kcal/mol) and 6CJI (−6.1 kcal/mol).

In conclusion, N-doped C-dots were successfully synthesized using Trillium govanianum root extract via a calcination approach. The green synthesis method proved superior to conventional chemical procedures in terms of biosafety, eco-friendliness, non-toxicity, and enhanced antifungal efficacy. The synthesized C-dots were comprehensively characterized for their structural and morphological properties using FTIR, XRD, Raman spectroscopy, and HRTEM. The antifungal potential of the C-dots was evaluated for their ability to inhibit biofilm formation in three isolates of Candida auris (C. auris NCCPF-470197, C. auris NCCPF-470200, and C. auris NCCPF-470203). The findings indicate that N-doped C-dots exhibit potent antifungal, antibiofilm, and antioxidant properties, making them highly versatile for both infection prevention and treatment. The superior fungicidal activity is attributed to the strong interaction between the C-dots and Candida auris, with the highest activity observed against C. auris NCCPF-470203, followed by C. auris NCCPF-470200 and C. auris NCCPF-470197. The molecular docking analysis showed that the C-dots exhibited highest binding score against 6M4C followed by 4LE8 and 6CJI. Furthermore, the study highlights the potential application of N-doped C-dots in wound care products such as bandages and band-aids, providing localized and sustained antifungal effects. The promising results of this study highlight the potential of N-doped C-dots in mitigating fungal diseases, reducing infection rates, and improving outcomes in vulnerable patient populations.
